title: Context
description: Request context, response helpers, cookies, headers and query params

blocks.text: Every middleware and handler receives a Context — one per request. It holds the request, route match, mutable state, and response helpers. There is no separate request/response object; the context is both.

rows: Captured route params from the matched pattern.
Per-request state shared across middleware and handlers.
Mutable per-request slot set by middleware, read by handlers.
The caught error, set on the _error page render.
Whether this is a partial (SPA fragment) request.

blocks.text: ctx.state is how middleware passes data downstream — set ctx.state.userContext in an auth middleware and every later handler reads it typed.
The context builds responses. Every helper merges ctx.headers first, so headers and cookies set earlier ride along automatically.

rows: Server-Sent Events stream — see the SSE page
HTML string via the registered engine

blocks.text: There is no ctx.render(). Page rendering happens in the segment pipeline via the registered engine, not from a context method. ctx.renderToString() is only for standalone markup (emails, fragments) outside the page flow.
ctx.headers is a mutable Headers instance merged into every response the context produces. Set headers anywhere in the request lifecycle and they persist — even across a redirect.
ctx.cookies reads from the request and writes Set-Cookie with correct append semantics. Secure defaults: httpOnly, sameSite: Strict, path: /.
ctx.query() reads the URL search params. Call it with a key for a single value (or undefined), or with no argument for the full record.

const q = ctx.query("q");        // string | undefined
const all = ctx.query();          // Record<string, string>
On API routes with a query schema, ctx.query() is typed and validated from the Zod schema rather than returning raw strings — see the API Routes page.
